analogy: Think of "Paths to Modernisation" through the chapter's own opening images: 153 Paths to Modernisation EAST ASIA at the beginning of the nineteenth century was dominated by China. The Qing dynasty, heir to a long tradition, seemed secure in its power, while Japan, a small island country, seemed to be locked in isolation. Yet, within a... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Paths to Modernisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 153 Paths to Modernisation EAST ASIA at the beginning of the nineteenth century was dominated by China.

The text explains that the Qing dynasty, heir to a long tradition, seemed secure in its power, while Japan, a small island country, seemed to be locked in isolation.

The text explains that yet, within a few decades China was thrown into turmoil unable to face the colonial challenge.

The text explains that the imperial government lost political control, was unable to reform effectively and the country was convulsed by civil war.

The text explains that japan on the other hand was successful in building a modern nation-state, creating an industrial economy and even establishing a colonial empire by incorporating Taiwan (1895) and Korea (1910).

The text explains that it defeated China, the land that had been the source of its culture and ideals, in 1894, and Russia, a European power, in 1905.

The text explains that the Chinese reacted slowly and faced immense difficulties as they sought to redefine their traditions to cope with the modern world, and to rebuild their national strength and become free from Western and Japanese control.

The text explains that they found that they could achieve both objectives – of removing inequalities and of rebuilding their country – through revolution.

The text explains that the Chinese Communist Party emerged victorious from the civil war in 1949.

The text explains that however, by the end of the 1970s Chinese leaders felt that the ideological system was retarding economic growth and development.

The text explains that this led to wide-ranging reforms of the economy that brought back capitalism and the free market even as the Communist Party retained political control.

The text explains that japan became an advanced industrial nation but its drive for empire led to war and defeat at the hands of the Anglo-American forces.

The text explains that the US Occupation marked the beginning of a more democratic political system and Japan rebuilt its economy to emerge by the 1970s as a major economic power.

The text explains that the Japanese path to modernisation was built on capitalist principles and took place within a world dominated by Western colonialism.

The text explains that japanese expansion was justified by the call to resist Western domination and liberate Asia.

The text explains that the rapid development underlined the strength of tradition in Japanese institutions and society, their ability to learn and the strength of nationalism.

The text explains that china is a vast continental country that spans many climatic zones; the core is dominated by three major river systems: the Yellow River (Huang He), the Yangtse River (Chang Jiang – the third longest river in the world) and the Pearl River.

The text explains that more than 50 per cent of the land area of the main islands is mountainous and Japan is situated in a very active earthquake zone.

The text explains that rice is the staple crop and fish the major source of protein.

The text explains that silk production helped the growth of a class of regional entrepreneurs who challenged the Tokugawa order, and when foreign trade started in 1859 Japan’s silk exports became a major source of profit for the economy struggling to compete with Western goods.

The text explains that the Emperor would be treated with reverence as he was considered a direct descendant of the Sun Goddess but he was also shown as the leader of westernisation.

The text explains that the military and the bureaucracy were put under the direct command of the emperor.

The text explains that to reduce population pressure the government actively encouraged migration, first to the northern island of Hokkaido, which had been a largely autonomous area where the indigenous people called the Ainu lived, and then to Hawaii and Brazil, as well as to the growing colonial empire of Japan.

The text explains that the rapid and unregulated growth of industry and the demand for natural resources such as timber led to environmental destruction.

The text explains that he was one of the core members of the Meirokusha, a society to promote Western learning.

The text explains that this pressure led the government to announce a constitution.

The text explains that agrarian reforms, the re-establishment of trade unions and an attempt to dismantle the zaibatsu or large monopoly houses that dominated the Japanese economy were also carried out.

The text explains that grass-roots pressure groups began to demand recognition of these problems as well as compensation for the victims.

The text explains that revolutionaries called for driving out the foreigners, who were controlling the country’s resources, to remove inequalities and reduce poverty.

The text explains that the Story of Korea Beginnings of Modernisation During the late nineteenth century, Korea’s Joseon Dynasty (1392–1910) faced internal political and social strife and increasing foreign pressure from China, Japan and the West.

The text explains that later that year, Chun became the president through an indirect election under the Yusin Constitution.

The text explains that as a result, citizens’ self-awareness about political rights grew, leading to demands for a constitutional amendment to allow direct election of the president.

The text explains that owing to these efforts, the Chun administration was forced to make a revision to the constitution, allowing direct elections.

The text explains that korean Democracy and the IMF Crisis As per the new constitution, the first direct election since 1971 was held in December 1987.

The text explains that meanwhile, under increasing neoliberalist pressure to open its market, the Kim administration joined the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) in 1996 and attempted to strengthen Korea’s international competitiveness.

The text explains that many of these are extracts for what historians call ‘primary sources’.

The text explains that different historians may use a given source-material to advance vastly different, even contradictory arguments about historical phenomena.

The text explains that the Scientific Revolution and the Origins of Modern Science, New York.

The text explains that theme Six l Trigger, Bruce G. and Wilcomb E.Washburn (eds).

The text explains that the Cambridge History of the Native Peoples of the Americas Vol.

The text explains that 186 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY Theme Seven l McClain, James L.

The text explains that a Modern History of Japan, New York: W.W.Norton & Co. l Daikichi, Irokawa.

The text explains that the Age of Hirohito: In Search of Modern Japan.

The text explains that oxford University Press, London l Spence, Jonathan.

The text explains that lectures on Modern Korean History:1945–1990, Dolbegae, Seoul. • Han, Yeong-Wo.

The text explains that kyongsaewon, Seoul. • The Association of Korean History Teachers.

The text explains that national Museum of Korean Contemporary History, Seoul • Korea University Center for Korean History.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Themes in World History, Chapter 7: Paths to Modernisation

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEME and 154 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Paths to Modernisation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Paths to Modernisation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THEME and 154 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
